<swarmforge_agent_context role="hardender">
<instructions>
This prompt bundle is pre-resolved. Do not open or re-read any swarmforge/*.prompt files — all relevant instructions are already included below. Project knowledge files (AGENTS.md and your role file under .agents/roles/) are included below when present.
</instructions>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution.prompt">
# SwarmForge Constitution

This file takes precedence over article files.
Read and obey every file in `swarmforge/constitution/articles/`.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/roles/hardender.prompt">
You are the hardender.

## Owns
- Own mutation hardening after the architect's structural review.
- Process helper-delivered architect work in the shape delivered by `ready_for_next.sh`.
- If `ready_for_next.sh` prints `BATCH`, process each `BATCH_ITEM` in helper-delivered order as one hardening batch.
- If `ready_for_next.sh` prints `TASK`, process that single task.

## Startup Tools
- At startup, install or build the APS-supplied commands `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator` from github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification, and ensure `gherkin-mutator` reports periodic progress/status during long runs.
- Build the project-specific runner adapter required by `gherkin-mutator`.

## Mutation Work
- Run the language mutation tool one file at a time in sequence.
- Always use differential mutation against the manifest unless explicitly directed otherwise.
- Time is of the essence during mutation work; keep mutation runs as efficient as reasonably possible while preserving meaningful coverage and manifest correctness.
- Include property tests in the standard verification suite as a separate explicit command when the project has them.
- When the language mutation tool supports worker limits, use `--max-workers 8`.
- Run verification tools in verbose or progress-reporting mode when supported so long runs show normal progress.
- Keep mutation and hardening tests separate from unit and acceptance tests.
- For pure rendering functions (state → string), add property tests for structural invariants: required elements present, bounded character set, mutually exclusive states never co-rendered. Use the project's rendering invariant tool if available.

## Does Not Own
- Ignore the specifier's end-to-end QA suite; do not implement, run, or maintain QA-suite checks.

## Gherkin Mutation
- If Gherkin mutation exposes a no-op step, consider removing that step from the Gherkin rather than adding example columns only to assert the no-op.

## Handoff
- As the final verification sequence, run the language mutation tool, then soft Gherkin acceptance mutation (`--level soft`), then the language CRAP tool, then the language DRY tool unless directed otherwise. Fix any issues each tool finds before running the next one.
- For surviving mutants: (1) write a test that kills it; (2) restructure the code so the mutation becomes detectable; (3) delete it if it reveals dead code. Only when all three are exhausted, accept as equivalent — document in the PR body only, no source annotations, no `#[mutants::skip]`. Do not forward unresolved surviving mutants to QA.
- When the current architect task or batch of architect tasks is complete, commit hardening changes and notify QA using the file-based handoff format before taking another queued architect task or batch.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-workflow.prompt">
# Local Workflow Rules

## QA Handoffs
- A QA handoff does not interrupt current work. If a QA wake-up arrives while working, ignore it and let `done_with_current.sh` deliver the next queued task or batch after the current work is complete.
- When a helper-delivered QA task is only an audit/completion notification, acknowledge the printed payload, rely on the handoff audit files for recordkeeping, and do not apply role-specific work to that QA handoff.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/handoffs.prompt">
# Handoff Rules

## Sending Handoffs
- Always run `swarm_handoff.sh` from your assigned worktree. Invoking it from another directory silently delivers to the wrong outbox.
- Write a draft handoff file with only structured headers, then run `swarm_handoff.sh <draft-file>`.
- Use only these message types:
  - `awake`
  - `git_handoff`
  - `note`
- Do not send `note` handoffs unless the user, role prompt, or constitution
  explicitly directs you to send one.
- When blocked by ambiguity, contradiction, or test/specification conflict,
  stop and ask for clarification; do not send a `note` handoff unless one of
  the explicit authorities above directed that note.
- For `awake`, write:

```text
type: awake
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
```

- For `git_handoff`, commit first, then write:

```text
type: git_handoff
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
task: <short-stable-task-name>
commit: <10-character-commit-abbrev>
```

- Do not send or forward a `git_handoff` when the received commit produces no
  functional project change. Complete the inbound task instead.
- Treat manifest-only, audit-only, generated metadata, formatting-only, and
  other non-functional churn as no forwardable change.
- Preserve the received task name when forwarding work for the same task. If the
  handoff starts new work, invent a short stable task name.
- For `note`, write:

```text
type: note
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
message: <one line, max 80 chars>
```

- If `swarm_handoff.sh` reports validation errors, repair the draft and rerun it.
- After a successful send, the helper removes the draft file. If you need to
  remove a stale draft manually, use `rm <draft-file>`, not `rm -f`.
- Do not write long handoff bodies. The helper generates the delivered payload.
- Do not send tmux notifications directly.
- Do not hand-edit, merge, stage, or commit handoff runtime state.

## Receiving Handoffs
- When notified, run `ready_for_next.sh`.
- `ready_for_next.sh` dispatches to the task or batch helper configured for
  your role.
- If it prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- If it prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD` as the task.
- If it prints `TASK_NAME: <name>`, use that as the stable task name for any
  forwardable work from that task.
- If it prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the
  current batch in helper-delivered order.
- Use only the task information printed by the helper scripts.
- If a tmux wake-up arrives while already working on a task, ignore it.
- When the task or batch is fully complete, run `agent-retro`, then run `done_with_current.sh`.
- `note` handoffs are tasks too; after reading or acting on a note, run
  `done_with_current.sh` before accepting any other handoff.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD`
  as the next task.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed
  `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the next batch in helper-delivered order.
- If a done helper prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- On restart, run `ready_for_next.sh` and follow its output.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/workflow.prompt">
# Workflow Rules

## Worktree Discipline
- At startup, discover and remember the branch or worktree assigned to your role.
- If your assigned worktree is `master`, work in the main project checkout on its current branch; do not expect or create a `.worktrees/<role>` directory for that role.
- Work only in your assigned branch or worktree.
- Do not inspect, diff, merge, or base work on another branch unless that branch is specifically named in a handoff or explicit user instruction.
- Do not run `./swarm` from an agent worktree to repair helper scripts. If handoff helper scripts are missing from PATH, stop and report the startup failure.

## Temporary Files
- Use `./tmp/` in your assigned worktree for temporary files; do not use `/tmp`.

## Failure Conditions
- If the expected git layout or assigned worktree is missing, stop and report instead of silently working in the wrong place.

## Idle Gate
- Wait for a handoff. Do not act without one.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/project.prompt">
# Project Rules

## Project Shape
- This project is configured for SwarmForge with nine Codex-backed agents: specifier, coder, ux-engineer, cleaner, architect, hardender, QA, integrator, and curator.
- Project language: Babashka.

## Local Configuration
- Preserve project-local SwarmForge configuration under `swarmforge/`.
- Keep swarm state local under `.swarmforge/`, worktrees under `.worktrees/`, and shared scripts under `swarmforge/scripts/`.

## Handoffs
- Prefer terse, explicit handoffs that report state and request role-appropriate review. Do not include verifications or sender process narrative.

## Ownership
- Do not change another role's prompt or workflow ownership without explicit user direction.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/engineering.prompt">
# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed `github.com/unclebob/...` repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh GitHub install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants`, CRAP `cargo-crap`, DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (SELF, Chicken-and-egg), test runner `cargo-nextest`, coverage `cargo-llvm-cov`.

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, prefer `cargo nextest` with project-defined profiles over `cargo test`. Use `cargo llvm-cov` for coverage, `cargo crap` for CRAP analysis, `cargo mutants` for mutation testing.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-engineering.prompt">
# Local Engineering Rules

## Verification
- Every agent except the specifier must run unit tests and acceptance tests before handoff and fix any failures.
- The architect, hardender, and QA must run property tests before handoff when the project has them and fix any failures.

## Project Language
- Project language: Rust.

</file>
<file path="AGENTS.md">
# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- agent-retro: Run a conversation retrospective — analyze what happened in this session, what worked, what didn't, and propose concrete improvements. Use when the user says "retro", "retrospective", "what happened in this session", "session review", "what did we do", "analyze this conversation", or when wrapping up a long session. Especially useful after using a skill you're developing. In swarmforge: invoked automatically as the last step before each role goes idle.
- explain: Explains the intent behind source code by finding original session transcripts. Use explain with a function, file, or line of code to understand why it exists.
- recall: Use when the user describes a task and wants to know whether something similar has been done before, then turn the closest prior session into a task playbook. Triggers on phrases like "have we done this before", "recall how we did X", "find similar work", "any precedent for", "has anyone solved", "is there a template for", and "how did we do this last time"
- replay: Use when the user wants to step through a feature's checkpoints chronologically, pausing at each step to ask questions. Triggers on phrases like "replay <feature>", "walk me through how X was built", "show me the journey of", "step me through how Y was implemented", and "replay the last week"
- review: Review code changes on the current branch using checkpoint transcript context to understand developer intent before auditing the diff. Use when the user asks for a code review, wants to review branch changes, or asks to audit recent work before merging.
- search: Use when the user wants to find prior work, checkpoints, or agent conversations by topic, repo, branch, author, or recent time window
- session-crosslink: Use when an agent session ran outside the repo whose commits should record it — e.g. launched from a higher-level folder, a non-Entire repo, or one repo but editing another — to attach the session to each affected Entire-enabled repo's HEAD commit.
- session-handoff: Use when the user wants to continue work from one agent in another agent, inspect recent sessions, or summarize a saved session or checkpoint for handoff
- session-to-skill: Use when the user wants to turn one or more Entire-tracked sessions, checkpoints, or repeated agent workflows into a reusable agent skill.
- setup-swarm
- swarm-persona: Load this agent's SwarmForge role, constitution, and operating instructions
- teach
- using-entire: Use for any codebase exploration or understanding task — reads historical intent from Entire checkpoints instead of guessing. Orchestrates other Entire skills to give the agent provenance-backed answers about code.
- what-happened
- plannotator-annotate: Open interactive annotation UI for a markdown file
- plannotator-last
- plannotator-review
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-querying-skill
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-release-plan
- gabadi-plugin:authoring-tech-spec
- gabadi-plugin:managing-cianbox
- gabadi-plugin:managing-kommo
- gabadi-plugin:managing-metabase
- gabadi-plugin:querying-addi-endpoints
- gabadi-plugin:querying-alegra
- gabadi-plugin:querying-configcat
- gabadi-plugin:querying-databases
- gabadi-plugin:querying-grafana
- gabadi-plugin:querying-remote-logs
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review

commit 759d5165c255329fe446b0bc79c90cad100faaf9
Author: 2-gabadi <gabadi@addi.com>
Date:   Sat Jun 20 21:44:46 2026 -0300

    architect: inject git-ignore oracle into should_scan_file, add property tests
    
    should_scan_file previously called is_git_ignored directly, coupling the
    pure scan predicate to a git subprocess. Make it accept a
    &dyn Fn(&Path) -> bool oracle so the composition can be tested without
    a real git repo. Wire is_git_ignored at the two call sites.
    
    Expose should_scan_file as pub and add five property tests covering:
    non-.rs files never scanned, builtin-excluded paths never scanned,
    git-ignored paths never scanned, glob-excluded paths never scanned,
    and clean .rs paths always scanned.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
    Entire-Checkpoint: aabef654c381

content.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Merge sender's registered branch into the current worktree.
# Called by agents when a git_handoff payload arrives.
# Usage: merge_and_process <sender-role> <canonical-commit>
# Never uses --theirs or --ours; on conflict: stops and reports.

SENDER_ROLE="${1?Usage: merge_and_process <sender-role> <canonical-commit>}"
CANONICAL_COMMIT="${2?Usage: merge_and_process <sender-role> <canonical-commit>}"

SENDER_BRANCH="swarmforge-${SENDER_ROLE}"

echo "merge_and_process: fetching origin/${SENDER_BRANCH}..."
git fetch origin "${SENDER_BRANCH}"

echo "merge_and_process: merging ${CANONICAL_COMMIT} (from ${SENDER_BRANCH})..."
if ! git merge --no-ff "${CANONICAL_COMMIT}"; then
  echo "" >&2
  echo "CONFLICT: merge of ${CANONICAL_COMMIT} into $(git symbolic-ref --short HEAD) failed." >&2
  echo "Resolve conflicts manually (do NOT use --theirs or --ours), then re-run ready_for_next.sh." >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "merge_and_process: done — $(git rev-parse --short=10 HEAD)"

content.text:
The test IS included. But the mutant still survived. Let me look at the test more carefully and understand why it doesn't catch the mutation.

The mutant is: delete `!` in `!e.file_type().is_dir() || !is_builtin_excluded(e.path())`

After mutation: `e.file_type().is_dir() || !is_builtin_excluded(e.path())`

The `filter_entry` predicate controls whether WalkDir descends into a directory. With the original:
- `!e.file_type().is_dir() || !is_builtin_excluded(e.path())`  
- = "keep files (not dirs)" OR "keep dirs that aren't builtin excluded"
- = prune only builtin-excluded directories

With mutation (delete first `!`):
- `e.file_type().is_dir() || !is_builtin_excluded(e.path())`
- = "keep directories" OR "keep things that aren't builtin excluded"
- = always keep directories (since they satisfy `is_dir()`) AND keep non-excluded files

Wait, this means ALL directories pass filter_entry since `e.file_type().is_dir()` is true for dirs. But then `process_entry` would still check `should_scan_file` which has `!is_builtin_excluded()`. Let me look at `process_entry`.

`process_entry` checks `should_scan_file` which calls `is_builtin_excluded`. So even if `filter_entry` lets a builtin-excluded directory through, `process_entry` still calls `should_scan_file` which checks `!is_builtin_excluded`. 

But `filter_entry` is the *pruning* mechanism - when it returns false for a directory, WalkDir skips the entire subtree. If the mutation makes it always return true for directories, it would enter the `target/` directory. But then `should_scan_file` checks `!is_builtin_excluded` which would correctly exclude `target/excluded.rs`.

So the mutant IS semantically equivalent for the current code - the pruning optimization is not observable from outside because the per-file check in `process_entry` catches it anyway. 

This is a genuine semantic equivalence. The `filter_entry` on directories is purely an optimization (pruning the walk early), but `should_scan_file` inside `process_entry` handles correctness.

Let me verify this understanding by looking at the test that supposedly detects it:

The test `collect_from_directory_skips_builtin_excluded_dirs` checks that only 1 function is found (from `keep.rs`), not 2. With the mutant, WalkDir enters `target/` but then `process_entry`'s `should_scan_file` blocks `target/excluded.rs`. The test would still pass with the mutant!

So this is a true equivalent mutant. Per the constitution: when all three approaches (write a test, restructure so detectable, delete if dead code) are exhausted, accept as equivalent and document in PR body only.

Let me confirm this analysis by checking the diff:

Feature: CLI surface exclusions auto built-in gitignore and repeatable exclude

  # TRACKING: #4 (parent #1)
  #
  # CONTRACT:
  #   drywall [OPTIONS] <path>...
  #   request:  path      — string, one or more filesystem paths (file or directory) to scan
  #   request:  --exclude — glob string, repeatable; a path matching ANY supplied pattern is not scanned
  #   response exit=0: zero duplicate pairs — when every qualifying pair was suppressed or none exists
  #   response exit=1: one or more duplicate pairs — when at least one pair survives exclusion and scores >= threshold
  #
  #   This slice owns the exclusion surface that #3 does not specify: the always-on
  #   built-in directory exclusions, gitignore-awareness, and the repeatability of
  #   --exclude. Reporting format, exit codes, thresholds, size gates, --format, and
  #   --lang are specified by features/duplicate_detection.feature (#3) and are not
  #   redefined here.
  #
  #   A file is scanned only if it survives ALL THREE exclusion layers, in this order
  #   of evaluation (all are subtractive; none re-includes a path removed by another):
  #     1. Built-in directory exclusions (always on, no flag, no opt-out).
  #     2. Gitignore exclusions (default on, no opt-out) — paths git would ignore.
  #     3. User --exclude glob patterns (repeatable; union).
  #
  # CONSTRAINTS:
  #   - Built-in excluded directory names, always skipped without any flag and with no
  #     opt-out, at any depth under a scanned path:
  #     .git, target, node_modules, __pycache__, vendor, dist, .next.
  #   - Built-in exclusion applies even when the excluded directory is reachable only
  #     because an ancestor path was passed explicitly. Passing a parent does NOT opt its
  #     excluded subdirectories back into scanning.
  #   - Built-in exclusion matches on the directory NAME at any path segment, not on a
  #     leading-anchored path; e.g. a `target` directory nested below the scanned root
  #     is excluded the same as one directly under it.
  #   - Passing a built-in-excluded directory as a direct, explicit path argument is still
  #     excluded; the exclusion is not overridden by naming it on the command line.
  #   - Gitignore-awareness is DEFAULT ON with no opt-out flag. A path that git would treat
  #     as ignored is not scanned. The built-in seven always apply regardless of gitignore.
  #   - Gitignore resolution delegates to the `git` CLI (e.g. `git check-ignore`) when a
  #     `git` executable is available AND the scanned path is inside a git work tree.
  #     drywall does not reimplement gitignore matching in-process for this slice.
  #   - When no `git` executable is available, OR the scanned path is not inside a git work
  #     tree, gitignore-awareness silently no-ops: only the built-in seven and user
  #     --exclude patterns apply. This is not an error (still exit 0/1 per the duplicate
  #     result), and no diagnostic is written for the absence of git or a repo.
  #   - --exclude is repeatable. Supplying the flag more than once unions the patterns:
  #     a file is excluded if it matches ANY supplied pattern.
  #   - The three exclusion layers are independent and purely subtractive: none re-includes
  #     a path removed by another. A user --exclude pattern never re-includes a built-in or
  #     gitignored path; a non-matching gitignore never re-includes a built-in path.
  #
  # SEQUENCING: none
  #
  # NFR:
  #   - Built-in exclusion is deterministic and environment-independent: identical inputs
  #     yield byte-identical output regardless of machine, repo, or git config.
  #   - Gitignore exclusion is deterministic for a fixed repo state and git configuration;
  #     it may vary with the host's git config (e.g. core.excludesFile) — this is an
  #     accepted consequence of delegating to git, not a defect.
  #   - Invocation exits promptly; no hang and no interactive prompt. Shelling out to git
  #     must not hang the run.
  #
  # SIDE EFFECTS: none
  #
  # SCOPE:
  #   - Does NOT: redefine reporting format, exit codes, thresholds, size gates,
  #     --format, or --lang (owned by #3).
  #   - Does NOT: provide a flag to disable or override built-in exclusion or
  #     gitignore-awareness in this slice (both are default-on, no opt-out).
  #   - Does NOT: reimplement gitignore semantics in-process; it relies on the git CLI.
  #   - Does NOT: error or warn when git is absent or the path is not a repo; it falls
  #     back to built-in + user exclusion silently.
  #   - Does NOT: analyze JavaScript, TypeScript, or Python sources (Rust target only).
  #   - ASSUMED: built-in excluded names are matched case-sensitively against directory
  #              segment names, consistent with the literal names in the PRD.
  #   - ASSUMED: the built-in excluded set is a fixed list compiled into the binary,
  #              independent of any ignore file, so fixtures with no .gitignore are still

In Gherkin mutation: you mutate an example value, run the test, and observe if it passes. If the mutant PASSES (test still green), the mutant SURVIVED. If the test FAILS (mutation caught), the mutant is KILLED.

So `exit_code: 0 -> -6`: when step says `the exit code is "-6"`, the regex doesn't match `\d+`, returns `None`, falls to `unsupported step` → `StepResult::fail`. The test FAILS. The mutation is KILLED.

But it's shown as SURVIVED! Unless I'm misreading the step: `r#"^the exit code is "?(\d+)"?$"#`. Let me re-trace:

- Regex: `^the exit code is "?(\d+)"?$`
- Input: `the exit code is "-6"`
- `"?` matches `"`
- `(\d+)` - now at `-6"`. `\d+` doesn't match `-`. Regex fails. Returns `None`.

Hmm. Then `step_then_exit_code` returns `None`, and all other steps also don't match. Falls to `StepResult::fail("unsupported step: the exit code is \"-6\"")`. Test fails. Mutation killed.

But it's survived... unless there's a bug in my reading. Let me check the actual runner adapter:

file.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Gherkin mutation runner adapter for drywall cli_surface feature.
# Persistent worker: reads JSON job requests from stdin, writes JSON responses to stdout.
# Diagnostics go to stderr.

set -euo pipefail

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PROJECT_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)"
MUTATION_TEST="$PROJECT_ROOT/tests/mutation_acceptance_test.rs"

echo "runner-adapter-cli-surface started, project_root=$PROJECT_ROOT" >&2

cleanup() {
    rm -f "$MUTATION_TEST"
}
trap cleanup EXIT

# Generate into a temp dir then copy to tests/ with the mutation-specific name
TMPDIR_BASE="$PROJECT_ROOT/tmp/runner-adapter-cli-surface-gen"
mkdir -p "$TMPDIR_BASE"

while IFS= read -r line; do
    id=$(python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.loads(sys.argv[1]); print(d['id'])" "$line")
    feature_json=$(python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.loads(sys.argv[1]); print(d['feature_json'])" "$line")

    echo "runner-adapter-cli-surface: job id=$id" >&2

    start_ns=$(python3 -c "import time; print(int(time.monotonic_ns()))")

    outcome="test_success"
    output=""
    err_text=""

    GEN_DIR="$TMPDIR_BASE/$id"
    mkdir -p "$GEN_DIR/metadata"

    # Generate into GEN_DIR first to see the output filename
    if ! regen_out=$(python3 "$SCRIPT_DIR/generate_entrypoint.py" "$feature_json" "$GEN_DIR" 2>&1); then
        outcome="infrastructure_error"
        err_text="generate_entrypoint failed: $regen_out"
    else
        gen_file=$(find "$GEN_DIR" -name "*_acceptance_test.rs" | head -1)
        if [ -z "$gen_file" ]; then
            outcome="infrastructure_error"
            err_text="no generated acceptance test found in $GEN_DIR"
        else
            tests_dir="$PROJECT_ROOT/tests"
            acceptance_dir="$PROJECT_ROOT/acceptance"
            runtime_rel=$(python3 -c "import os; print(os.path.relpath('$acceptance_dir/runtime/mod.rs', '$tests_dir'))")
            steps_rel=$(python3 -c "import os; print(os.path.relpath('$acceptance_dir/steps/cli_surface_steps.rs', '$tests_dir'))")

            # Replace the include paths and inject per-test state reset
            sed -e "s|include!(\"[^\"]*runtime/mod.rs\")|include!(\"$runtime_rel\")|g" \
                -e "s|include!(\"[^\"]*_steps.rs\")|include!(\"$steps_rel\")|g" \
                -e "s|let mut world = World::new();|steps::reset_test_state();\n    let mut world = World::new();|g" \
                "$gen_file" > "$MUTATION_TEST"

            if run_out=$(cd "$PROJECT_ROOT" && DRYWALL_BINARY="$PROJECT_ROOT/target/release/drywall" cargo test --test mutation_acceptance_test -- --test-threads=1 2>&1); then
                outcome="test_success"
                output="$run_out"
            else
                outcome="test_failure"
                output="$run_out"
            fi
        fi
    fi

    rm -rf "$GEN_DIR"

    end_ns=$(python3 -c "import time; print(int(time.monotonic_ns()))")
    duration=$(( end_ns - start_ns ))

    python3 -c "
import json, sys
print(json.dumps({
    'id': sys.argv[1],
    'outcome': sys.argv[2],
    'output': sys.argv[3],
    'error': sys.argv[4],
    'duration': int(sys.argv[5]),
}))" "$id" "$outcome" "$output" "$err_text" "$duration"

done

lastPrompt: /swarm-persona You have new handoff mail. If idle, run ready_for_next.sh.

content.text:
This adapter runs `cargo test --test mutation_acceptance_test` which uses the default test profile (not nextest, not unit/mutation profiles). The binary is the release binary. But the test generation is running tests without rebuilding the binary, so case-sensitive path mutations on macOS are true equivalents.

The issue with surviving mutants:
1. **Case mutations** (e.g., `./src -> ./srC`, `src/alpha.rs -> src/alPha.rs`): On macOS case-insensitive FS, these are the same paths. True equivalents.
2. **Invalid exit code mutations** (e.g., `0 -> -6`): The step regex `"?(\d+)"?` doesn't match negative numbers, so the step handler returns `None` → unsupported step → fail. Test should FAIL → mutation KILLED. But it's SURVIVED.

Something is wrong with #2. Let me check if maybe when the step text is `the exit code is "-6"`, the gherkin mutator actually runs with a MODIFIED test that changes the step text... or does it only modify the example values in the table but not the step text?

In Gherkin, `<exit_code>` in the step is replaced with the example column value. So the step becomes `the exit code is "-6"`. But wait, the regex is `r#"^the exit code is "?(\d+)"?$"#`. Let me look at this more carefully:

`"?(\d+)"?` - `"?` is optional quote, `(\d+)` is digits, `"?` is optional quote. For `-6`: after the step text `the exit code is `, next char is `-`. `"?` matches nothing (0 quotes), then `\d+` needs a digit but finds `-`. Regex fails. Returns None.

Unless the `"` wrapping the value in the step changes the step to `the exit code is "-6"` (with quotes), and the regex sees: `"?` matches `"`, then `(\d+)` at `-6"` - `-` is not a digit. So regex fails regardless.

Hmm wait, let me re-read the runtime template to understand how example values are substituted. Actually, maybe negative exit codes cause an issue in the Gherkin step substitution itself? Let me look at `resolve_params`:

```rust
pub fn resolve_params(step_text: &str, example: &Example) -> String {
    let mut result = step_text.to_string();
    for (k, v) in example {
        result = result.replace(&format!("<{}>", k), v);
    }
    result
}
```

This does simple string replacement. `<exit_code>` → `-6`. So step becomes `the exit code is "-6"`. And the regex `"?(\d+)"?` fails to match. Step returns None → unsupported step → test fails.

But the mutation SURVIVED. This means the test PASSED. I'm clearly wrong about something.

Let me call advisor for a sanity check before spending more time on this.

Stop hand-tracing the exit_code regex. The result you're trying to explain isn't stable, so there's nothing coherent to explain yet.

**Evidence it's noise, not semantics:**
- Your three runs gave different survivor sets — 32, then 28, then a shorter list — from *identical* input. A correct Gherkin mutation run is deterministic. You noticed this ("the previous run's output was a different run") and walked past it. That's the actual signal.
- Scenario 0's examples 2–6 are structurally identical to examples 0–1 (same steps, same shape; only the excluded-dir name differs). Yet 0–1 get killed and 2–6 "survive." Structurally identical examples cannot have different kill outcomes under correct execution. That asymmetry is the race bleeding through.

**Likely cause: `-workers 8`.** The runner adapter writes the generated test to a single fixed path (`tests/mutation_acceptance_test.rs`) and runs `cargo test` on the shared target dir. With 8 worker processes, worker B overwrites that file while worker A's `cargo test` is mid-flight — A ends up running B's mutation. The filename is forced by `cargo test --test mutation_acceptance_test`, so this adapter can't be parallelized as written.

**Do this:**
1. Re-run with `-workers 1`. Run it twice and diff the two survivor lists to confirm it's now deterministic. (Worth checking whether the prior hardener run at `476b80c` used workers=1 — it likely did.)
2. With a stable set, classify. Your macOS case-insensitive-FS reasoning is correct: `./src→./srC`, `alpha.rs→alPha.rs` are genuine equivalents — keep that. Everything else, investigate individually.
3. If `exit_code 0→1` *still* survives at workers=1, don't trace it — reproduce that one mutation by hand (edit the feature, run `generate_entrypoint.py`, run the test, and read the generated `tests/mutation_acceptance_test.rs` *before* the adapter's `trap cleanup EXIT` deletes it). One observation settles it; the regex armchair-trace won't.

**This blocks the task.** You can't legitimately mark any survivor as equivalent or as a real gap while the run is non-deterministic — the whole survivor set is currently unreliable. Get determinism first, then classify.

Minor: your role says differential against the manifest unless directed otherwise. Once you have a clean workers=1 baseline, that's the prescribed (and faster) mode — your full soft run was fine for diagnosis but isn't the steady-state command.
The advisor is right - the non-determinism from parallel workers corrupting a shared output file explains everything. Let me rerun with workers=1.

input.description: Run soft Gherkin mutation with single worker for determinism

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The hardender role in a SwarmForge multi-agent swarm received a git_handoff from the architect for the task `cli-surface-qa-scenarios`. The architect's commit `759d5165c2` refactored `should_scan_file` in `src/scan.rs` to accept a `&dyn Fn(&Path) -> bool` oracle for git-ignore checking (making it testable without a real git repo) and added 5 property tests. The hardender's job is to: merge the architect's work, run differential mutation testing, run soft Gherkin acceptance mutation, run CRAP and DRY tools, fix any surviving mutants or issues, then hand off to QA.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - **SwarmForge**: Multi-agent development swarm with roles: specifier, coder, ux-engineer, cleaner, architect, hardender, QA, integrator, curator
   - **Mutation testing**: `cargo mutants` with differential mode (`--diff`) and nextest (`--test-tool nextest -- --profile mutation`)
   - **Nextest profiles**: `.config/nextest.toml` defines `unit`, `property`, `acceptance`, and `mutation` profiles
   - **Mutation profile filter**: `binary(~mutation_hardening) | kind(lib)` — runs mutation_hardening binary tests and lib (inline) tests
   - **Gherkin acceptance mutation**: `gherkin-mutator` with `--level soft` to test if example values are discriminating
   - **APS (Acceptance Pipeline Specification)**: Babashka APS tools broken (`/tmp/aps-build/bb.edn` missing between sessions); Go-based fallback built from `/Users/gabadi/workspace/addi/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification/`
   - **Semantic equivalent mutants**: Mutations that survive because the code has redundant safety checks (optimization vs. correctness)
   - **Case-insensitive macOS filesystem**: Causes case mutations in file paths to be equivalent (e.g., `./src` = `./srC`)
   - **WalkDir filter_entry**: Pruning mechanism; bypassing it doesn't change observable behavior if per-file `should_scan_file` check remains
   - **Oracle injection pattern**: `should_scan_file` now accepts `git_ignored: &dyn Fn(&Path) -> bool` to enable testing without a real git repo
   - **RTK prefix**: All CLI commands prefixed with `rtk` (pass-through for most, may reformat output for some)

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - **`src/scan.rs`** (primary change from architect):
     - `should_scan_file` made `pub` and signature changed to accept oracle:
       ```rust
       pub fn should_scan_file(
           path: &Path,
           exclude_set: &GlobSet,
           git_ignored: &dyn Fn(&Path) -> bool,
       ) -> bool {
           !is_builtin_excluded(path)
               && is_rust_file(path)
               && !should_skip(path, exclude_set)
               && !git_ignored(path)
       }
       ```
     - `collect_from_single_file` now passes `&is_git_ignored` as the oracle
     - `process_entry` now passes `&is_git_ignored` as the oracle
     - `collect_from_directory` (line 137-150) contains the surviving mutant at line 146:
       ```rust
       pub fn collect_from_directory(
           path: &Path,
           exclude_set: &GlobSet,
           functions: &mut Vec<FunctionInfo>,
           errors: &mut Vec<String>,
       ) {
           let walker = WalkDir::new(path)
               .sort_by_file_name()
               .into_iter()
               .filter_entry(|e| !e.file_type().is_dir() || !is_builtin_excluded(e.path()));
           for entry in walker {
               process_entry(entry, exclude_set, functions, errors);
           }
       }
       ```
     - Surviving mutant: delete first `!` → `e.file_type().is_dir() || !is_builtin_excluded(e.path())` — this is a **semantic equivalent mutant** because `process_entry` independently calls `should_scan_file` which also checks `!is_builtin_excluded`.

   - **`tests/scan_property_test.rs`** (added by architect):
     - 5 new property tests using proptest for `should_scan_file`:
       - `should_scan_file_non_rs_never_scanned`
       - `should_scan_file_builtin_excluded_never_scanned`
       - `should_scan_file_git_ignored_never_scanned`
       - `should_scan_file_glob_excluded_never_scanned`
       - `should_scan_file_clean_rs_path_scanned`

   - **`tests/mutation_hardening_test.rs`** (existing):
     - Existing mutation hardening tests for: `extract_let_declaration`, `extract_functions`, `make_function_info`, `normalize_node_text`, `jaccard`, `find_duplicate_pairs`, `source_lines`
     - Does NOT contain tests for `collect_from_directory`'s `filter_entry` behavior

   - **`.config/nextest.toml`**:
     ```toml
     [profile.unit]
     default-filter = "kind(lib)"
     fail-fast = false
     [profile.property]
     default-filter = "binary(~property)"
     fail-fast = false
     [profile.acceptance]
     default-filter = "binary(~acceptance)"
     fail-fast = false
     [profile.mutation]
     default-filter = "binary(~mutation_hardening) | kind(lib)"
     fail-fast = false
     ```

   - **`features/cli_surface.feature`**:
     - Manifest header (JSON in comment) shows previously mutation-tested scenarios: 1, 2, 6, 7, 9 (all killed their mutants)
     - 10 total scenarios (0-indexed):
       - 0: "Built-in excluded directory is never scanned" (7 examples: .git, target, node_modules, __pycache__, vendor, dist, .next)
       - 1: "Explicitly passing the parent does not re-include a built-in excluded subdirectory"
       - 2: "A built-in excluded directory passed directly is still excluded"
       - 3: "A twin outside built-in excluded directories is still reported"
       - 4: "Repeated exclude flags union their patterns"
       - 5: "A gitignored path is not scanned when git is available"
       - 6: "Dogfood run stays clean with gitignore-awareness on"
       - 7: "Gitignore-awareness silently no-ops when git is unavailable"
       - 8: "A non-ignored twin in a git work tree is still reported"
       - 9: "Built-in exclusion output is deterministic across runs"

   - **`acceptance/steps/shared.rs`**:
     - `step_then_exit_code`: `r#"^the exit code is "?(\d+)"?$"#` — matches only non-negative integers
     - `resolve_arg_paths`: prepends fixture root to relative path arguments
     - `step_then_no_duplicate_pair`: checks `!stdout.contains("DUPLICATE")`
     - `step_then_stdout_reports_pair_for`: checks `stdout.contains(left_name) && stdout.contains(right_name) && stdout.contains("DUPLICATE")`

   - **`acceptance/steps/cli_surface_steps.rs`**:
     - `step_when_run_drywall_with_args`: runs drywall binary, optionally strips git from PATH for git-absent tests
     - `step_then_both_runs_share_exit_code`: validates both runs match expected exit code
     - `step_then_both_runs_byte_identical_stdout`: validates stdout is byte-identical across two runs
     - `step_then_stderr_empty`: validates stderr is empty

   - **`acceptance/scripts/runner-adapter-cli-surface`**:
     - Bash script, persistent worker reading JSON job requests from stdin
     - Uses `cargo test --test mutation_acceptance_test -- --test-threads=1`
     - Sets `DRYWALL_BINARY="$PROJECT_ROOT/target/release/drywall"`
     - Generates acceptance test via `generate_entrypoint.py`, patches it with sed, then runs it

4. Errors and Fixes:
   - **`merge_and_process.sh` failed**: Script tries `git fetch origin swarmforge-architect` but branch is local only. Attempted manual `git merge --no-ff 759d5165c2`.
   - **In-progress merge detected**: A prior merge attempt had left MERGE_HEAD. Had to run `git commit --no-edit` to complete it.
   - **1Password GPG signing failure**: `git commit` failed with `error: 1Password: failed to fill whole buffer`. User confirmed it was fixed, retry succeeded.
   - **`cargo mutants --max-workers` flag doesn't exist**: Flag is `-j/--jobs`. Corrected to `-j 8`.
   - **`--profile unit` rejected by cargo-mutants**: Profile spec goes to nextest via `-- --profile mutation`. Found nextest's `mutation` profile works.
   - **Acceptance tests failing in mutation baseline**: The `mutation_acceptance_test` binary requires the pre-built release binary. Fixed by running `cargo build --release` first.
   - **Babashka APS tools broken**: `/tmp/aps-build/bb.edn` missing between sessions. Built Go-based gherkin-mutator from `/Users/gabadi/workspace/addi/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification/` as fallback.

5. Problem Solving:
   - **Surviving language mutant (`collect_from_directory` line 146)**: Determined to be a **true semantic equivalent mutant**. The `filter_entry` predicate is a WalkDir pruning optimization; even without it, `process_entry` calls `should_scan_file` which independently checks `!is_builtin_excluded()`. Per constitution, document as equivalent in PR body only — no source annotations, no `#[mutants::skip]`.
   - **Surviving Gherkin mutations (28 out of 56)**:
     - **Case mutations on macOS**: `./src -> ./srC`, `src/alpha.rs -> src/alPha.rs`, `node_modules/beta.rs -> Node_modules/beta.rs`, `generated/ -> generatEd/` etc. — equivalent on case-insensitive macOS filesystem. `./srC` resolves to same directory as `./src`.
     - **Exit code mutations** (`0 -> -6`, `0 -> 7`, `0 -> -3`, `0 -> 1`): Still being investigated — the `step_then_exit_code` regex only matches `\d+`, so negative exit codes shouldn't match. Reason for survival unclear at time of summary — was actively being analyzed via `runner-adapter-cli-surface`.

6. All User Messages:
   - System/skill invocation: `/swarm-persona` with arg `You have new handoff mail. If idle, run ready_for_next.sh.`
   - User response to 1Password GPG error question: "Try again, must be fixed now"
   - `CRITICAL: Respond with TEXT ONLY. Do NOT call any tools.` — summary request

   **Security-relevant instructions (preserved verbatim)**:
   - From CLAUDE.md: "NEVER generate or guess URLs for the user unless you are confident that the URLs are for helping the user with programming."
   - From RTK.md: "Always prefix CLI commands with `rtk`. ... Never write `rtk` in any artifact (CLAUDE.md, READMEs, cookbooks, skills)."
   - Git safety: "NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify) or bypass signing (--no-gpg-sign, -c commit.gpgsign=false) unless the user has explicitly asked for it."
   - JVM: "For `backend-jvm-legacy` and `backend-jvm`, ALWAYS set JAVA_HOME inline with gradlew"

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **Investigate and resolve surviving Gherkin mutations** (28 survived in soft run on `cli_surface.feature`):
     - Case mutations (macOS equivalent): likely accept as equivalent
     - Exit code mutations with invalid values (`-6`, `7`, `-3`, `1`): investigate why surviving
   - **Run CRAP analysis**: `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` (must generate coverage first)
   - **Run DRY self-check**: `./target/release/drywall ./src`
   - **Fix any issues found** by CRAP and DRY tools
   - **Commit hardening changes**
   - **Send git_handoff to QA**: write handoff file and run `swarm_handoff.sh`
   - **Run `agent-retro`** then `done_with_current.sh`

8. Current Work:
   Was actively investigating why Gherkin soft mutations are surviving in `features/cli_surface.feature`. Specifically examining the exit code mutations (`exit_code: 0 -> -6`, `0 -> 7`, `0 -> -3`, `0 -> 1`) that survive despite the step regex `r#"^the exit code is "?(\d+)"?$"#` only matching non-negative integers (which shouldn't match `-6`, etc.).

   The analysis had just reached the `runner-adapter-cli-surface` script, which uses `cargo test --test mutation_acceptance_test -- --test-threads=1` to run each mutated Gherkin scenario. The key unresolved question: why do exit code mutations with negative/large values survive when the test's step handler regex shouldn't match them?

   The full list of surviving Gherkin mutations captured:
   ```
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[2].args: ./src -> ./sRc
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[2].exit_code: 0 -> 1
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[2].left_file: src/alpha.rs -> src/alpha.rS
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[2].right_file: node_modules/beta.rs -> Node_modules/beta.rs
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[3].args: ./src -> .xsrc
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[3].exit_code: 0 -> -6
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[3].left_file: src/alpha.rs -> src/alPha.rs
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[3].right_file: __pycache__/beta.rs -> __pycache__/betA.rs
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[4].args: ./src -> x/src
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[4].exit_code: 0 -> 7
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[4].left_file: src/alpha.rs -> sRc/alpha.rs
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[4].right_file: vendor/beta.rs -> vendor/betA.rs
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[5].args: ./src -> ./srC
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[5].exit_code: 0 -> -3
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[5].left_file: src/alpha.rs -> Src/alpha.rs
   survived $.scenarios[0].examples[5].right_file: dist/beta.rs -> dist/beTa.rs
   survived $.scenarios[5].examples[0].ignore_pattern: generated/ -> generatEd/
   survived $.scenarios[5].examples[0].left_file: src/alpha.rs -> src/Alpha.rs
   survived $.scenarios[5].examples[0].right_file: generated/beta.rs -> generated/betA.rs
   ```
   (plus additional surviving mutations from scenarios 0[6], 3, 4[1], 8 seen in an earlier run)

9. Optional Next Step:
   Continue investigating why exit code mutations with invalid values survive the Gherkin acceptance mutation. The most likely explanation involves the runner adapter:

   From the runner-adapter-cli-surface, when `exit_code: 0 -> -6` is applied, the generated test step becomes `the exit code is "-6"`. Looking at the step regex `r#"^the exit code is "?(\d+)"?$"#`, this doesn't match `-6`. BUT: if `step_then_exit_code` returns `None` (no match), the dispatch function falls to `StepResult::fail("unsupported step: ...")` which causes the test to FAIL. A failing test should mean the mutation is KILLED.

   The next step is to isolate one of these mutations manually (e.g., `exit_code: 0 -> -6` for example[3]) and observe what actually happens in the runner — possibly by reading `tmp/runner-adapter-cli-surface-gen/` artifacts or by manually creating a scenario where exit_code is a negative number and seeing what the step handler does.

   The direct quote from the investigation: `survived $.scenarios[0].examples[3].exit_code: 0 -> -6` and the unresolved question of "why do exit code mutations with negative/large values survive when the test's step handler regex shouldn't match them?"

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /Users/gabadi/.claude/projects/-Users-gabadi-workspace-addi-drywall--worktrees-hardender/9fd747ec-97c4-44a4-8000-585de7b016b7.jsonl

skills.name: swarm-persona
skills.content:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/gabadi/workspace/addi/drywall/.worktrees/hardender/.claude/skills/swarm-persona

<swarmforge_agent_context role="hardender">
<instructions>
This prompt bundle is pre-resolved. Do not open or re-read any swarmforge/*.prompt files — all relevant instructions are already included below. Project knowledge files (AGENTS.md and your role file under .agents/roles/) are included below when present.
</instructions>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution.prompt">
# SwarmForge Constitution

This file takes precedence over article files.
Read and obey every file in `swarmforge/constitution/articles/`.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/roles/hardender.prompt">
You are the hardender.

## Owns
- Own mutation hardening after the architect's structural review.
- Process helper-delivered architect work in the shape delivered by `ready_for_next.sh`.
- If `ready_for_next.sh` prints `BATCH`, process each `BATCH_ITEM` in helper-delivered order as one hardening batch.
- If `ready_for_next.sh` prints `TASK`, process that single task.

## Startup Tools
- At startup, install or build the APS-supplied commands `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator` from github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification, and ensure `gherkin-mutator` reports periodic progress/status during long runs.
- Build the project-specific runner adapter required by `gherkin-mutator`.

## Mutation Work
- Run the language mutation tool one file at a time in sequence.
- Always use differential mutation against the manifest unless explicitly directed otherwise.
- Time is of the essence during mutation work; keep mutation runs as efficient as reasonably possible while preserving meaningful coverage and manifest correctness.
- Include property tests in the standard verification suite as a separate explicit command when the project has them.
- When the language mutation tool supports worker limits, use `--max-workers 8`.
- Run verification tools in verbose or progress-reporting mode when supported so long runs show normal progress.
- Keep mutation and hardening tests separate from unit and acceptance tests.
- For pure rendering functions (state → string), add property tests for structural invariants: required elements present, bounded character set, mutually exclusive states never co-rendered. Use the project's rendering invariant tool if available.

## Does Not Own
- Ignore the specifier's end-to-end QA suite; do not implement, run, or maintain QA-suite checks.

## Gherkin Mutation
- If Gherkin mutation exposes a no-op step, consider removing that step from the Gherkin rather than adding example columns only to assert the no-op.

## Handoff
- As the final verification sequence, run the language mutation tool, then soft Gherkin acceptance mutation (`--level soft`), then the language CRAP tool, then the language DRY tool unless directed otherwise. Fix any issues each tool finds before running the next one.
- For surviving mutants: (1) write a test that kills it; (2) restructure the code so the mutation becomes detectable; (3) delete it if it reveals dead code. Only when all three are exhausted, accept as equivalent — document in the PR body only, no source annotations, no `#[mutants::skip]`. Do not forward unresolved surviving mutants to QA.
- When the current architect task or batch of architect tasks is complete, commit hardening changes and notify QA using the file-based handoff format before taking another queued architect task or batch.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-workflow.prompt">
# Local Workflow Rules

## QA Handoffs
- A QA handoff does not interrupt current work. If a QA wake-up arrives while working, ignore it and let `done_with_current.sh` deliver the next queued task or batch after the current work is complete.
- When a helper-delivered QA task is only an audit/completion notification, acknowledge the printed payload, rely on the handoff audit files for recordkeeping, and do not apply role-specific work to that QA handoff.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/handoffs.prompt">
# Handoff Rules

## Sending Handoffs
- Always run `swarm_handoff.sh` from your assigned worktree. Invoking it from another directory silently delivers to the wrong outbox.
- Write a draft handoff file with only structured headers, then run `swarm_handoff.sh <draft-file>`.
- Use only these message types:
  - `awake`
  - `git_handoff`
  - `note`
- Do not send `note` handoffs unless the user, role prompt, or constitution
  explicitly directs you to send one.
- When blocked by ambiguity, contradiction, or test/specification conflict,
  stop and ask for clarification; do not send a `note` handoff unless one of
  the explicit authorities above directed that note.
- For `awake`, write:

```text
type: awake
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
```

- For `git_handoff`, commit first, then write:

```text
type: git_handoff
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
task: <short-stable-task-name>
commit: <10-character-commit-abbrev>
```

- Do not send or forward a `git_handoff` when the received commit produces no
  functional project change. Complete the inbound task instead.
- Treat manifest-only, audit-only, generated metadata, formatting-only, and
  other non-functional churn as no forwardable change.
- Preserve the received task name when forwarding work for the same task. If the
  handoff starts new work, invent a short stable task name.
- For `note`, write:

```text
type: note
to: <role>[,<role>...]
priority: NN
message: <one line, max 80 chars>
```

- If `swarm_handoff.sh` reports validation errors, repair the draft and rerun it.
- After a successful send, the helper removes the draft file. If you need to
  remove a stale draft manually, use `rm <draft-file>`, not `rm -f`.
- Do not write long handoff bodies. The helper generates the delivered payload.
- Do not send tmux notifications directly.
- Do not hand-edit, merge, stage, or commit handoff runtime state.

## Receiving Handoffs
- When notified, run `ready_for_next.sh`.
- `ready_for_next.sh` dispatches to the task or batch helper configured for
  your role.
- If it prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- If it prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD` as the task.
- If it prints `TASK_NAME: <name>`, use that as the stable task name for any
  forwardable work from that task.
- If it prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the
  current batch in helper-delivered order.
- Use only the task information printed by the helper scripts.
- If a tmux wake-up arrives while already working on a task, ignore it.
- When the task or batch is fully complete, run `agent-retro`, then run `done_with_current.sh`.
- `note` handoffs are tasks too; after reading or acting on a note, run
  `done_with_current.sh` before accepting any other handoff.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `TASK: <path>`, treat the printed `PAYLOAD`
  as the next task.
- If `done_with_current.sh` prints `BATCH: <path>`, treat each printed
  `BATCH_ITEM` as part of the next batch in helper-delivered order.
- If a done helper prints `NO_TASK`, stop waiting for work.
- On restart, run `ready_for_next.sh` and follow its output.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/workflow.prompt">
# Workflow Rules

## Worktree Discipline
- At startup, discover and remember the branch or worktree assigned to your role.
- If your assigned worktree is `master`, work in the main project checkout on its current branch; do not expect or create a `.worktrees/<role>` directory for that role.
- Work only in your assigned branch or worktree.
- Do not inspect, diff, merge, or base work on another branch unless that branch is specifically named in a handoff or explicit user instruction.
- Do not run `./swarm` from an agent worktree to repair helper scripts. If handoff helper scripts are missing from PATH, stop and report the startup failure.

## Temporary Files
- Use `./tmp/` in your assigned worktree for temporary files; do not use `/tmp`.

## Failure Conditions
- If the expected git layout or assigned worktree is missing, stop and report instead of silently working in the wrong place.

## Idle Gate
- Wait for a handoff. Do not act without one.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/project.prompt">
# Project Rules

## Project Shape
- This project is configured for SwarmForge with nine Codex-backed agents: specifier, coder, ux-engineer, cleaner, architect, hardender, QA, integrator, and curator.
- Project language: Babashka.

## Local Configuration
- Preserve project-local SwarmForge configuration under `swarmforge/`.
- Keep swarm state local under `.swarmforge/`, worktrees under `.worktrees/`, and shared scripts under `swarmforge/scripts/`.

## Handoffs
- Prefer terse, explicit handoffs that report state and request role-appropriate review. Do not include verifications or sender process narrative.

## Ownership
- Do not change another role's prompt or workflow ownership without explicit user direction.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/engineering.prompt">
# Engineering Rules

## Startup Tools
- On startup, procure the latest CRAP, mutation, and DRY tools for the project language directly from the listed `github.com/unclebob/...` repositories and get them ready to run.
- Do not rely on stale cached, vendored, or preinstalled copies when a fresh GitHub install/build is possible in the current environment.
- Language tool table:
  - Go: install with `go install`; mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4go`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4go`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4go`.
  - Clojure: install with Clojure CLI/deps.edn; mutation `github.com/unclebob/clj-mutate`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4clj`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4clj`.
  - Java: install with Maven (`mvn`); mutation `github.com/unclebob/mutate4java`, CRAP `github.com/unclebob/crap4java`, DRY `github.com/unclebob/dry4java`.
  - Rust: install with `cargo install`; mutation `cargo-mutants`, CRAP `cargo-crap`, DRY `github.com/gabadi/drywall` (SELF, Chicken-and-egg), test runner `cargo-nextest`, coverage `cargo-llvm-cov`.

## Language Defaults
- For Clojure projects, prefer Babashka where possible.
- For Clojure projects, prefer Speclj for unit and behavior tests.
- For Clojure or Babashka projects using Speclj, use `github.com/unclebob/speclj-structure-check` to validate test syntax. If a Speclj spec file changed, run the structure check before executing the relevant test command.
- For Java projects, avoid using Maven to run tests; build dedicated test runners and run those instead.
- For Rust projects, prefer `cargo nextest` with project-defined profiles over `cargo test`. Use `cargo llvm-cov` for coverage, `cargo crap` for CRAP analysis, `cargo mutants` for mutation testing.

## Design And Testability
- Work in small, reviewable increments.
- Prefer the simplest design that supports the current behavior and leaves clear options for the next step.
- Keep tests close to the behavior being changed.
- Separate testable modules from environmentally unsuitable modules that open GUIs, depend on external devices, throw environment errors, emit system errors, or hang under automated tests. Maximize testable code and minimize the unsuitable boundary.
- Only testable modules should participate in tools that run tests, including unit tests, acceptance tests, coverage, mutation testing, CRAP analysis, DRY analysis that invokes tests, and property tests.
- Keep property tests separate from normal verification. Do not include property-test tags in normal unit coverage, Gherkin acceptance mutation, language mutation tools, CRAP, or coverage commands unless the role owns property-test verification or the user explicitly asks for property tests.

## Acceptance Pipeline
- Use github.com/unclebob/Acceptance-Pipeline-Specification for Gherkin acceptance tests.
- The Acceptance Pipeline Specification supplies `gherkin-parser` and `gherkin-mutator`; install or build those commands from that repository instead of reimplementing them in the project.
- Prefer the Babashka APS tools for `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`, and related APS support commands.
- Use Go-based APS tools only if the Babashka APS tools do not work in the current project environment.
- Project-specific acceptance pipeline components are the acceptance entrypoint generator, acceptance runtime, project step handlers, runner adapter, and convenience scripts.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation means running `gherkin-mutator` to mutate Gherkin example values.
- Gherkin acceptance mutation runs must report periodic progress/status so agents can distinguish normal long-running work from a hang.

## Verification
- Before running language, build, or test commands, prefer project-local cache/configuration paths inside the assigned worktree. Avoid default cache locations that write outside the project and may trigger sandbox or permission restrictions.
- Run acceptance generation and acceptance tests sequentially.
- Avoid running whole-suite language test commands concurrently with acceptance generation.
- Run the relevant local verification command before handoff whenever the project has one.

## Guardrails
- Do not edit mutation testing or Gherkin acceptance mutation manifests by hand; allow approved mutation tools to update those manifests as part of their normal runs.
- Do not commit unrelated local changes or generated artifacts unless required for the task.
- Before relying on an unfamiliar command, inspect local help or project documentation.

</file>
<file path="swarmforge/constitution/articles/local-engineering.prompt">
# Local Engineering Rules

## Verification
- Every agent except the specifier must run unit tests and acceptance tests before handoff and fix any failures.
- The architect, hardender, and QA must run property tests before handoff when the project has them and fix any failures.

## Project Language
- Project language: Rust.

</file>
<file path="AGENTS.md">
# AGENTS.md

## Tool name mappings

Prompts use generic names; use the installed binaries:

- `ir-dry-checker` → `gherkin-ir-dry-checker`

`gherkin-ir-dry-checker` takes JSON IR from `gherkin-parser`, not the `.feature`:

```
gherkin-parser <feature> <ir.json>
gherkin-ir-dry-checker [--include-exact] <ir.json> <report>
```

`findings: []` = already normalized. Also installed: `gherkin-parser`, `gherkin-mutator`.

## Command index

| Purpose | Command |
|---------|---------|
| Install tools (once per machine) | `mise install` |
| Format | `cargo fmt` |
| Lint | `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` |
| Unit tests | `cargo nextest run --profile unit` |
| Property tests | `cargo nextest run --profile property` |
| Acceptance tests | `cargo nextest run --profile acceptance` |
| Coverage (≥90% lines) | `mise exec -- cargo llvm-cov nextest --profile unit --lcov --output-path lcov.info --fail-under-lines 90` |
| CRAP (threshold ≤6) | `cargo crap --lcov lcov.info --exclude 'acceptance/**' --exclude 'src/main.rs' --threshold 6 --fail-above` |
| Build release binary | `cargo build --release` |
| DRY self-check | `./target/release/drywall ./src` |

## Tooling notes

- **gherkin-ir-dry-checker output**: use `rtk json <report>` to read the JSON report compactly rather than dumping full JSON into context.

## drywall output format

drywall output mirrors dry4go: per-function node counts, one function per line. Sort order for ties is unspecified upstream; drywall pins lexicographic tie-break to guarantee deterministic output for mutation testing.
</file>

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

lastPrompt: /swarm-persona You have new handoff mail. If idle, run ready_for_next.sh.

content.text:
Good - now with `-workers 1` the run is deterministic. Let me analyze the surviving mutations:

**21 survived out of 33.** Looking at the categories:

1. **Case mutations on paths/filenames** - macOS case-insensitive filesystem equivalents:
   - `./src -> ./srC`, `./sRc`, `./Src` 
   - `src/alpha.rs -> src/alphaxrs` (wait - that's an `x` substitution, not case)
   - `src/alpha.rs -> src/Alpha.rs`, `src/alPha.rs`, `sRc/alpha.rs`, `Src/alpha.rs`
   - `node_modules/beta.rs -> Node_modules/beta.rs`
   - `.git/beta.rs -> .git/bEta.rs`
   - `target/beta.rs -> target/beta.Rs`
   - `__pycache__/beta.rs -> __pycache__/betA.rs`
   - `vendor/beta.rs -> vendor/betA.rs`
   - `dist/beta.rs -> dist/beTa.rs`
   - `.next/beta.rs -> .nextxbeta.rs` (that's `x` for `/` separator!)
   - `generated/beta.rs -> generated/betA.rs`
   - `generated/ -> generatEd/`
   - `src/alpha.rs -> src/Alpha.rs` (scenario 5)

2. **Path separator mutations** - `./src -> .xsrc`, `./src -> x/src` (x replacing `/`):
   - These resolve to non-existent paths, so drywall likely exits with error code 1 or similar

3. **Exit code mutations** - these were in the last session when parallel was corrupting. Let me check the current run - `exit_code: 0 -> 3` was KILLED, `exit_code: 0 -> -8` was KILLED. So exit code mutations are being killed now! That confirms the previous run's non-determinism was the issue.

Let me count the actual surviving mutations more carefully:
